item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely cool!

By the way, if you call the Crom discord bot just the phrase ?? scp, this shows up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely worth it. Honestly, SCPs based around numbers mess me up, as I'm not great with numbers, but this is a good one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a complaint about the actual layout of the file.
I read these at work, and the firewall stops certain things, like most images, so while I can assume from the comments here that this is a very clever and well thought file, I can't read it like some files. I'll have to read this on my phone or personal computer, but it is a bit of a disappointment that whatever interesting layout is used can't make it readable for me.
Hoping it is worth the hype I see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Out of deference to the mods
- Because this is a long one

I think this article belies the author's understanding about mathematics, much to the detriment of the piece. It vaguely brushes against the notions of Set Theory both in justification and execution, and yet fails to demonstrate even a basic understanding of any of the applications. You reference ordinal numbers but don't particularly consider the implications of what ordering means. Much less any discussion on cardinality. There are still outstanding unsolved problems on the various cardinalities of any given set. Setting aside the more fundamental issues of this, containment is trivial with any uncountably infinite set. Literally endless.

More to the point…

When a number is given a non-numerical symbol to represent it, such as g, SCP-𝕐 will still affect the underlying value, but the symbol itself will remain unaffected.

What do you think numbers are? The crux of this article relies on the idea that numbers are some sort of fundamental component of mathematics that enjoy a privileged position, but they aren't. They're not even particularly special, and any given relevancy is dependent on your choice of starting axioms, field, and ring.

The idea of separating numbers from names strikes the layman as natural but it's a wholly arbitrary distinction. The concept of numbers as a meaningful label only arises from needing labels in an algebraic structure which is anyways constructed through the relations of operations and identities.

The 'discovery' of this is similarly out of left field -

If there are infinite numbers of these countable sets of things which exist, then it serves to reason there is a countable set which contains things which do not.

Given that this nowhere remotely comes close to anything resembling the burden of proof for a theory worth its salt. And as alluded to above, why does it have to even be countable? There are uncountably infinite sets of uncountably infinite things, there are null sets - the construction of a set is arbitrary and meaning is inferred from its usefulness as a mathematical tool or how interesting it actually is. How does one even 'uncover' this? You can't look for things that are 'missing' because you've already constructed your algebraic structures in such a way that anything that is 'missing' is absent by design.

And how does the existence of this not invalidate the underlying operations and identities that comprise a set? If we even just restrict ourselves to the natural numbers, a nice ordered set - then there's quite a few numbers that, if 'disappeared', would invalidate the natural numbers' designation as a set entirely! The article is wholly unequipped to deal with the implications of that. Heck, how does this set interact with Russell's paradox?

I'm sure there's more I could ramble about here - but I will leave it at this:

NGC 5789 is now actually NGC 5788.9999999, and then everyone just rounds it up.

What does it round up to if the number 5789 doesn't exist and can't be conceptualised? And how is a series of infinitely repeating 9s after the decimal not equal to the next number?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any definite article in English is a reference to a singular finite value, I am compelled to note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that depends on whether this set is really an extension on how math works or just an anomaly that sort of covers the numbers from usage.

If it's the first one, then you can reason that there must be ways, by which you can calculate with this set. Although it would probably be risky.

The second possibility means somebody/something just stops you from using these numbers, making any calculations impossible. Calling it a set would then just be for convenience sake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome concept, great execution. The tests don't drag on but they're interesting and I kinda live appendixes showcasing what has happened and can happen if containment fails. The containment procedures are a big part of the appeal, they're very creative. Overall a great math SCP, the only gripe I have with it is that "eating" numbers every day makes a set of numbers seem a bit like a classic monster and the fact that it seems kinda implausible to me that only one guy has come up with the concept before and that no numbers were lost to the pieces of paper where the concept was written alongside, say, dates. Still a fun read, so +1

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: Rounds it up to what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely interesting. Though early in the article they said that they would have an number at the bottom and there obviously isn't one. Hell preferably that would be changed everyday but what can you do.

specific increment upwards to rating numerical value.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: Exactly. Had they not done that, and possibly until they did that, NGC 5789 DIDN'T exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It ISN'T purely concept, I don't think. The number 5789? Gone? The six-billion digit numbers they have erased each day? Gone. It's not succeeding in containment per se, but rather… It's like the worms that are probably far far underneath your house. They don't interfere, but just because they are completely out of sight and mind doesn't mean they don't exist.

Secondly, I think it's more so the name of the Site is a symbol. It's not algebra, it's just the name. Same if they named it Site-Circle or Site-Headphones. They just picked a cool looking symbol that means Jupiter.

And last but not least, 5789 is gone. When they first contained it, it was labelled as SCP-5789. Because of that, SCP-5789 got rid of the number five-thousand-seven-hundred-eighty-nine. So, the only way they can even mark it is as SCP-Y, or SCP-����.

(not originally poster, but hope this helped)

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
Exactly this.

Also:

The galaxy NGC 5789 would likely fall into a similar fate as the D-class.

That would be an awesome tale. You should write that, Clevehammer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No numerals, ordinals, or other words indicative of a singular, finite value may be used in any documentation related to this anomaly.

perhaps 'specific' rather than 'singular' ?

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: This is explained in the last section. The Foundation fixed this with rounding. NGC 5789 is now actually NGC 5788.9999999, and then everyone just rounds it up.

The concept is intriguing. However, once a number that small cosmologically (in this case, 5789) is missing, everything would collapse, not just computers. The galaxy NGC 5789 would likely fall into a similar fate as the D-class. In fact, it's non-existence would be known almost immediately by the public. What is the homeless population in Quebec in 2015? 5789. What did Ronald Reagan deliver on April 6, 1988? Proclamation 5789. What is the median income of black female led suburban families in 1977? $5789 USD. How many people in Assam, India were tested for Covid-19 as of April 22, 2020? 5789. And that's not to mention that some roads have a No. 5789. Calculating 7×527 would also yield 5789.

No. Such a anomaly would be immediately known, and the veil would be shredded by a mathematical collapse. Streisand-style.

Therefore, it's a -1 from me. You need to address the effects it had on existing knowledge.

item.title: ummmm
item.content:encoded: a indicates a specific finite value

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

I downvoted, wrote out a long couple of paragraphs to explain why, and in rereading to fuel my critique discovered something that swept away its basis. Upvoted and now I'm on the lookout for more. Nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what would happen if it was fed a single digit number… Would all numbers with that digit in them vanish too? For example, SCP-Y is fed the number 7. Would 17, 27,37, 70 and other numbers with 7 in them disappear too?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think this is limited to the set of real numbers, but strictly speaking the chance that an element chosen at random from the set of all possible numbers would ever be used by humanity is exactly 0%, provided humanity doesn't find some way to use every number, in which case the chance is 100% and we're screwed anyway. This would break proof by induction, for example, if 𝕐 ever ate any integer (which it did). I love the skip, but there's really no way to isolate the damage from an anomaly like this. All of mathematics is inextricably tied to all of the rest of mathematics. You break one thing, and you break everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A good article, but I have some opinions.

1. The anomaly is a purely concept. Why does just to feed it make the containment succeed?

2. This sentence is a bit confusing to me.

"When a number is given a non-numerical symbol to represent it, such as g, SCP-𝕐 will still affect the underlying value, but the symbol itself will remain unaffected"

Firstly, I thought ♃ is affected too. However, later I understand it references an algebraic use. ♃ is visual alternative use. So, I think the sentence needs to differentiate these two.

3. The anomaly's object number is SCP-����. Is this intended to describe the anomaly's nature?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's such a unique idea! I really liked it! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, I dig it.

This may just be me, but I found the “feeding the anomaly a D-Class” test to be unnecessary. None of the rest of the article worries about it affecting people in a physical way, and the horror/problem with covering up the loss of a 4 digit number in modern technical society was more than enough for me.

Still gets a +1, but what I see as an out of place trope dinged it a bit for me.

item.title: I am such a jerk...
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block




If you must do that, please at least put it in a collapsible. ~ Conwell

All of these numbers no longer exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is no Site-5 Antimemetics Division integer between 5,788 and 5,790.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah this feels just like a Series 1 scip wrapped up in modern prose. I knew 033 would be mentioned. I like it. +1

item.title: Excuse my immature imagination
item.content:encoded:
First of all, +1. Beautiful. But it makes me wonder about the potential of its anomaly, and what the foundation might be planning.

For example, at or before year 5789, what is the foundation planning? Would they make the world switch to a new calendar (like Holocene calendar, which is the calendar we use today but each year is added by 10000), or just skip a year?

Also, what would happen if its fed a non-base-10 number? For example, if it is "fed" base-12 5A (base-10 70), would it delete base-10 70, or not understand the context? Or, if it is "fed" base-12 100, would it delete base-10 100 or base-10 144? Does this SCP have the ability to distinguish between those contexts, and if it does, is this SCP immune to base conversion?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1… but a small gripe.

If several days pass without being "fed", SCP-𝕐 consumes a number at random, resulting in potential catastrophic consequences for financial, computing, and other mathematics-derivative fields of science.

Theoretically, this shouldn't be an issue. Let's say we establish a certain range of numbers (presumably real numbers) that humanity has, does, or will ever use. i.e., this range could be (-10 googols to 10 googols), for example. Because the real numbers are infinite, there is still a practically 100% chance that SCP-𝕐 will consume a number outside of this established range.

Maybe specify that SCP-𝕐 is more likely to consume a number relatively close to 0 so that the danger is clear? Just a suggestion. Love the math SCP, though. Great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nom nom numbers tasty

item.title: Re: Missed a number
item.content:encoded: Thanks for pointing this one out! It really showcases how difficult containing this thing is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely delightful. Excellent hard-science/mathematics SCP that is still totally intuitive while being simultaneously a mindfuck. Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Malice that the solution to losing a four digit number needs to be considerably more robust, but that's my only complaint. +1

item.title: Missed a number
item.content:encoded:
SCP-𝕐 was first theorized by famous mathematician

"first" is the ordinal for the number One, which i think its a pretty important number not to include in the documentation. I recommend swapping that out for "originally". Other than that nitpick, really fun and cool article! love me a good cool infohazard

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm having the same trouble seeing the actual skip, but I did manage to read it using the link above. It's great, as is the format, although IMO it ends too abruptly and I'd like some more explanation of how the Foundation dealt with the breach described at the end. Still, a great idea executed well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing I want to say is that the terminal style this SCP uses is really good. A lot of SCPs use this sort of sci-fi unrealistic terminal style that doesn't really mesh with reality, but this one is exactly like an old DOS computer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd love to read this post but all I'm getting is two exit system messages that I can't select. Am sad

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My phone and every browser on my computer (chrome, firefox, edge, IE) all do this. Chrome gives the following console errors when I expand the collapsible:

 Uncaught DOMException: Blocked a frame with origin "http://www.scp-wiki.net" from accessing a cross-origin frame. at http://www.scp-wiki.net/common--javascript/resize-iframe.html?0.7782295854484627:16:15

EDIT: I looked at the source for the main page, and pulling out the HTML seems to work just fine. You can paste it in an HTML file and view it locally, but I've also uploaded the source to jsfiddle so you can fiew it from this link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is exactly what happens, on both phone and chromebook. Its frustrating me because this has never been an issue before

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does the collapsible change to "Exit System" but nothing's inside? Mine's doing that as well; it's possible neither of our devices display Wikidot's embedded HTML blocks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do you access it? I click the "connect via remote session" thing and nothing happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very Nice.

+𝕐

I'm definitely gonna steal that code for something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
by creating an algorithm that sort of…rounds everything upward or downward by a certain amount so computers don't notice it's missing

This isn't how this works. This isn't how anything works. You can't… you can't just fix something like this with an 'algorithm' — you'd not only need to install it on every single computer system, but also every 7-segment display, every microcontroller, every teeny tiny sensor in every piece of lab equipment, every physical list of that many instructions. This would bring down the //world.

Filling in the gaps of a missing four digit number is a wildly ambitious thing to do, and something that, if it had to be attempted, deserves much more than 'an algorithm' in way of explanation, and way more writing than four paragraphs. Even if you dismiss it with some hand-wavy amnestics and advanced software-editing paratech, those things need to be properly addressed in-universe, because right now… I can make myself believe that numbers are erased from reality, but I can't believe that the solution you gave me would be enough. And I think it says a lot that that's where my suspension of disbelief breaks down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I presume that while impossible due to the numbers no longer existing, the cardinal value that the non existent numbers had could still be added together, subtracted, multiplied, etc, right?

Like, there are hypothetical sets of all sets (even though every set couldn't fit in a set like how a box of all boxes would have to contain itself) which logically must include the set of non-existent cardinals, and there's weird math for similar stuff so, it should stand to reason that there's a number line/plane/something which doesn't exist, but yet contains all the absurdly large nonexistent numbers which the Foundation has fed it, and a system of non-existent input-outputs that can be applied to the thing, right? Like, the plane of where all the missing socks go, but for numbers?

I don't even know what I'm saying anymore, maybe I'm looking into this too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cleaning up the loss of this one number takes round-the-clock work of a team of mathematicians and digital engineers.

oops

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's exactly why I chose this slot! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, for making an interesting excuse for not using ACS lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the formatting, but otherwise this was how I felt about it too. It's an interesting idea, but it was explicitly stated that this could be potentially a Big Problem in the containment procedures, and the story progression was more or less people repeating 'yeah, this could be a Big Problem' without any twists or turns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a part of me that's hoping that the item number is a "why is 6 afraid of 7? / because seven ate nine" joke.

Fantastic counterpart to -033!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
one integer

I see what you did there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OH SHIT

But yeah, that shouldn't be there, I thought I got all of them. Fixing now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To clarify, my complaint about padding was because my phone has the Sandbox 3 site blocked, so the border image wasn't loading; thanks for the fixes to the headers, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: scared to upvote, as to lose another number, but i'll risk it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending of this came across as little more than an attempt to reiterate just how dangerous the anomaly is, which really isn't that interesting. When combined with the unnecessary and intrusive formatting, my problems with this article outweighed the admittedly pretty interesting anomalous effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea that the number 5789 doesn't exist in that universe. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good article. Good work with the unique styling/format, helps greatly with the execution of the article.

+[a number that is greater than an amount of nothing by one integer]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No numerals, ordinals, or other words indicative of a singular, finite value may be used in any documentation related to this anomaly.

INPUT: All the digits of one number, gg, followed immediately by the digits of a second number, hh, are concatenated into a single number […]

Oh shit.

In all honesty, this is a really great skip. Really gives me some Department of Miscommunications vibes that I very much dig. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit, this is so fucking good.

This article has the roots of a memorable hard-science Series 1 SCP wrapped in the modern complexity of a Series 3 or 4 article. It's sophisticated but not to the point that it's technobabble. The css theme is an all-around nice touch.

+1 forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed some of the mobile issues!

item.title: Re: Bravo!
item.content:encoded: I don't see why not! this article is a watered down version of Turbo Vision Theme.

item.title: Bravo!
item.content:encoded:
This is fucking amazing. I could rant for hours about that format.
+1 (+100 if I could)
Oh hey, can I use that format with the windows 98 screen thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How does this react to the concept of imaginary numbers?
